topics.title: Introduction

content_blocks.text: Chapter 1: Essential Ideas
content_blocks.html: Chapter 1: Essential Ideas

content_blocks.text: Your alarm goes off and, after hitting snooze once or twice, you pry yourself out of bed. You make a cup of coffee to help you get going, and then you shower, get dressed, eat breakfast, and check your phone for messages.
content_blocks.html: Your alarm goes off and, after hitting snooze once or twice, you pry yourself out of bed. You make a cup of coffee to help you get going, and then you shower, get dressed, eat breakfast, and check your phone for messages.

content_blocks.text: On your way to school, you stop to fill your car's gas tank, almost making you late for the first day of chemistry class. As you find a seat in the classroom, you read the question projected on the screen: Welcome to class! Why should we study chemistry?
content_blocks.html: On your way to school, you stop to fill your car's gas tank, almost making you late for the first day of chemistry class. As you find a seat in the classroom, you read the question projected on the screen: Welcome to class! Why should we study chemistry?

content_blocks.text: Do you have an answer? You may be studying chemistry because it fulfills an academic requirement, but if you consider your daily activities, you might find chemistry interesting for other reasons. Most everything you do and encounter during your day involves chemistry.
content_blocks.html: Do you have an answer? You may be studying chemistry because it fulfills an academic requirement, but if you consider your daily activities, you might find chemistry interesting for other reasons. Most everything you do and encounter during your day involves chemistry.

You make a cup of coffee to help you get going, and then you shower, get dressed, eat breakfast, and check your phone for messages. On your way to school, you stop to fill your car's gas tank, almost making you late for the first day of chemistry class. As you find a seat in the classroom, you read the question projected on the screen: Welcome to class! Why should we study chemistry? Do you have an answer? You may be studying chemistry because it fulfills an academic requirement, but if you consider your daily activities, you might find chemistry interesting for other reasons. Most everything you do and encounter during your day involves chemistry.
topics.clean_html:
Chapter 1: Essential Ideas

Your alarm goes off and, after hitting snooze once or twice, you pry yourself out of bed. You make a cup of coffee to help you get going, and then you shower, get dressed, eat breakfast, and check your phone for messages.

On your way to school, you stop to fill your car's gas tank, almost making you late for the first day of chemistry class. As you find a seat in the classroom, you read the question projected on the screen: Welcome to class! Why should we study chemistry?

Do you have an answer? You may be studying chemistry because it fulfills an academic requirement, but if you consider your daily activities, you might find chemistry interesting for other reasons. Most everything you do and encounter during your day involves chemistry.

topics.title: Chemistry in Context

content_blocks.text: 1.1 Chemistry in Context
content_blocks.html: 1.1 Chemistry in Context

outcomes: Outline the historical development of chemistry
Provide examples of the importance of chemistry in everyday life
Describe the scientific method
Differentiate among hypotheses, theories, and laws
Provide examples illustrating macroscopic, microscopic, and symbolic domains
content_blocks.html:
• Outline the historical development of chemistry
  • Provide examples of the importance of chemistry in everyday life
  • Describe the scientific method
  • Differentiate among hypotheses, theories, and laws
  • Provide examples illustrating macroscopic, microscopic, and symbolic domains

content_blocks.text: Throughout human history, people have tried to convert matter into more useful forms. Our Stone Age ancestors chipped pieces of flint into useful tools and carved wood into statues and toys.
content_blocks.html: Throughout human history, people have tried to convert matter into more useful forms. Our Stone Age ancestors chipped pieces of flint into useful tools and carved wood into statues and toys.

content_blocks.text: Humans began to practice chemistry when they learned to control fire and use it to cook, make pottery, and smelt metals. Subsequently, they began to separate and use specific components of matter.
content_blocks.html: Humans began to practice chemistry when they learned to control fire and use it to cook, make pottery, and smelt metals. Subsequently, they began to separate and use specific components of matter.

content_blocks.text: Attempts to understand the behavior of matter extend back for more than 2500 years. As early as the sixth century BC, Greek philosophers discussed a system in which water was the basis of all things.
content_blocks.html: Attempts to understand the behavior of matter extend back for more than 2500 years. As early as the sixth century BC, Greek philosophers discussed a system in which water was the basis of all things.

topics.raw_text: 1.1 Chemistry in Context  Throughout human history, people have tried to convert matter into more useful forms. Our Stone Age ancestors chipped pieces of flint into useful tools and carved wood into statues and toys. Humans began to practice chemistry when they learned to control fire and use it to cook, make pottery, and smelt metals. Subsequently, they began to separate and use specific components of matter. Attempts to understand the behavior of matter extend back for more than 2500 years. As early as the sixth century BC, Greek philosophers discussed a system in which water was the basis of all things.
topics.clean_html:
1.1 Chemistry in Context

  • Outline the historical development of chemistry
  • Provide examples of the importance of chemistry in everyday life
  • Describe the scientific method
  • Differentiate among hypotheses, theories, and laws
  • Provide examples illustrating macroscopic, microscopic, and symbolic domains

Throughout human history, people have tried to convert matter into more useful forms. Our Stone Age ancestors chipped pieces of flint into useful tools and carved wood into statues and toys.

Humans began to practice chemistry when they learned to control fire and use it to cook, make pottery, and smelt metals. Subsequently, they began to separate and use specific components of matter.

Attempts to understand the behavior of matter extend back for more than 2500 years. As early as the sixth century BC, Greek philosophers discussed a system in which water was the basis of all things.

topics.title: Phases and Classification of Matter

content_blocks.text: 1.2 Phases and Classification of Matter
content_blocks.html: 1.2 Phases and Classification of Matter

outcomes: Describe the basic properties of each physical state of matter
Classify matter as an element, compound, homogeneous mixture, or heterogeneous mixture
Distinguish between mass and weight
Apply the law of conservation of matter
content_blocks.html:
• Describe the basic properties of each physical state of matter
  • Classify matter as an element, compound, homogeneous mixture, or heterogeneous mixture
  • Distinguish between mass and weight
  • Apply the law of conservation of matter

content_blocks.text: Matter is defined as anything that occupies space and has mass, and it is all around us. Mass and volume are measures of the amount of matter present in a substance.
content_blocks.html: Matter is defined as anything that occupies space and has mass, and it is all around us. Mass and volume are measures of the amount of matter present in a substance.

content_blocks.text: The three most common states or phases of matter are solid, liquid, and gas. A fourth state of matter, plasma, occurs naturally in the interiors of stars.
content_blocks.html: The three most common states or phases of matter are solid, liquid, and gas. A fourth state of matter, plasma, occurs naturally in the interiors of stars.

content_blocks.text: A solid has a definite shape and a definite volume. Liquids have a definite volume but take the shape of their container. Gases have neither a definite shape nor a definite volume.
content_blocks.html: A solid has a definite shape and a definite volume. Liquids have a definite volume but take the shape of their container. Gases have neither a definite shape nor a definite volume.

topics.raw_text: 1.2 Phases and Classification of Matter  Matter is defined as anything that occupies space and has mass, and it is all around us. Mass and volume are measures of the amount of matter present in a substance. The three most common states or phases of matter are solid, liquid, and gas. A fourth state of matter, plasma, occurs naturally in the interiors of stars. A solid has a definite shape and a definite volume. Liquids have a definite volume but take the shape of their container. Gases have neither a definite shape nor a definite volume.
topics.clean_html:
1.2 Phases and Classification of Matter

  • Describe the basic properties of each physical state of matter
  • Classify matter as an element, compound, homogeneous mixture, or heterogeneous mixture
  • Distinguish between mass and weight
  • Apply the law of conservation of matter

Matter is defined as anything that occupies space and has mass, and it is all around us. Mass and volume are measures of the amount of matter present in a substance.

The three most common states or phases of matter are solid, liquid, and gas. A fourth state of matter, plasma, occurs naturally in the interiors of stars.

A solid has a definite shape and a definite volume. Liquids have a definite volume but take the shape of their container. Gases have neither a definite shape nor a definite volume.

topics.title: Physical and Chemical Properties

content_blocks.text: 1.3 Physical and Chemical Properties
content_blocks.html: 1.3 Physical and Chemical Properties

outcomes: Identify properties of matter as physical or chemical
Give examples of extensive and intensive properties
content_blocks.html:
• Identify properties of matter as physical or chemical
  • Give examples of extensive and intensive properties

content_blocks.text: Properties of matter fall into one of two categories. If the property depends on the amount of matter present, it is an extensive property. Mass and volume are examples of extensive properties.
content_blocks.html: Properties of matter fall into one of two categories. If the property depends on the amount of matter present, it is an extensive property. Mass and volume are examples of extensive properties.

content_blocks.text: An intensive property is a property of matter that depends only on the type of matter in a sample, not on the amount. Color, temperature, and solubility are examples of intensive properties.
content_blocks.html: An intensive property is a property of matter that depends only on the type of matter in a sample, not on the amount. Color, temperature, and solubility are examples of intensive properties.

content_blocks.text: Physical properties include characteristics such as color, odor, density, melting point, boiling point, and hardness. Chemical properties describe the ability of a substance to undergo changes that transform it into a different substance.
content_blocks.html: Physical properties include characteristics such as color, odor, density, melting point, boiling point, and hardness. Chemical properties describe the ability of a substance to undergo changes that transform it into a different substance.

topics.raw_text: 1.3 Physical and Chemical Properties  Properties of matter fall into one of two categories. If the property depends on the amount of matter present, it is an extensive property. Mass and volume are examples of extensive properties. An intensive property is a property of matter that depends only on the type of matter in a sample, not on the amount. Color, temperature, and solubility are examples of intensive properties. Physical properties include characteristics such as color, odor, density, melting point, boiling point, and hardness. Chemical properties describe the ability of a substance to undergo changes that transform it into a different substance.
topics.clean_html:
1.3 Physical and Chemical Properties

  • Identify properties of matter as physical or chemical
  • Give examples of extensive and intensive properties

Properties of matter fall into one of two categories. If the property depends on the amount of matter present, it is an extensive property. Mass and volume are examples of extensive properties.

An intensive property is a property of matter that depends only on the type of matter in a sample, not on the amount. Color, temperature, and solubility are examples of intensive properties.

Physical properties include characteristics such as color, odor, density, melting point, boiling point, and hardness. Chemical properties describe the ability of a substance to undergo changes that transform it into a different substance.

topics.title: Measurements

content_blocks.text: 1.4 Measurements
content_blocks.html: 1.4 Measurements

outcomes: Explain the metric system
Define and use SI base units
Convert between units using dimensional analysis
content_blocks.html:
• Explain the metric system
  • Define and use SI base units
  • Convert between units using dimensional analysis

content_blocks.text: Measurements provide the macroscopic information that is the basis for most of the hypotheses, theories, and laws that describe the behavior of matter and energy in both the macroscopic and microscopic domains of chemistry.
content_blocks.html: Measurements provide the macroscopic information that is the basis for most of the hypotheses, theories, and laws that describe the behavior of matter and energy in both the macroscopic and microscopic domains of chemistry.

content_blocks.text: Every measurement provides three kinds of information: the size or magnitude of the measurement (a number); a standard of comparison for the measurement (a unit); and an indication of the uncertainty of the measurement.
content_blocks.html: Every measurement provides three kinds of information: the size or magnitude of the measurement (a number); a standard of comparison for the measurement (a unit); and an indication of the uncertainty of the measurement.

content_blocks.text: The SI system defines seven base units: meter (length), kilogram (mass), second (time), ampere (electric current), kelvin (temperature), mole (amount of substance), and candela (luminous intensity).
content_blocks.html: The SI system defines seven base units: meter (length), kilogram (mass), second (time), ampere (electric current), kelvin (temperature), mole (amount of substance), and candela (luminous intensity).

topics.subject: chemistry

topics.raw_text: 1.4 Measurements  Measurements provide the macroscopic information that is the basis for most of the hypotheses, theories, and laws that describe the behavior of matter and energy in both the macroscopic and microscopic domains of chemistry. Every measurement provides three kinds of information: the size or magnitude of the measurement (a number); a standard of comparison for the measurement (a unit); and an indication of the uncertainty of the measurement. The SI system defines seven base units: meter (length), kilogram (mass), second (time), ampere (electric current), kelvin (temperature), mole (amount of substance), and candela (luminous intensity).
topics.clean_html:
1.4 Measurements

  • Explain the metric system
  • Define and use SI base units
  • Convert between units using dimensional analysis

Measurements provide the macroscopic information that is the basis for most of the hypotheses, theories, and laws that describe the behavior of matter and energy in both the macroscopic and microscopic domains of chemistry.

Every measurement provides three kinds of information: the size or magnitude of the measurement (a number); a standard of comparison for the measurement (a unit); and an indication of the uncertainty of the measurement.

The SI system defines seven base units: meter (length), kilogram (mass), second (time), ampere (electric current), kelvin (temperature), mole (amount of substance), and candela (luminous intensity).

topics.title: Measurement Uncertainty, Accuracy, and Precision

content_blocks.text: 1.5 Measurement Uncertainty, Accuracy, and Precision
content_blocks.html: 1.5 Measurement Uncertainty, Accuracy, and Precision

outcomes: Define accuracy and precision
Distinguish between exact and measured numbers
Use significant figures in calculations
content_blocks.html:
• Define accuracy and precision
  • Distinguish between exact and measured numbers
  • Use significant figures in calculations

content_blocks.text: Counting is the only type of measurement that is free from uncertainty, provided the number of objects being counted does not change while counting and the result is less than infinity.
content_blocks.html: Counting is the only type of measurement that is free from uncertainty, provided the number of objects being counted does not change while counting and the result is less than infinity.

content_blocks.text: Accuracy refers to how closely a measurement aligns with the correct or true value. Precision refers to how closely repeated measurements under unchanged conditions show the same results.
content_blocks.html: Accuracy refers to how closely a measurement aligns with the correct or true value. Precision refers to how closely repeated measurements under unchanged conditions show the same results.

content_blocks.text: Significant figures convey the uncertainty in a measurement. The digits in a measurement include all certain digits plus one uncertain digit.
content_blocks.html: Significant figures convey the uncertainty in a measurement. The digits in a measurement include all certain digits plus one uncertain digit.

Accuracy refers to how closely a measurement aligns with the correct or true value. Precision refers to how closely repeated measurements under unchanged conditions show the same results. Significant figures convey the uncertainty in a measurement. The digits in a measurement include all certain digits plus one uncertain digit.

1.5 Measurement Uncertainty, Accuracy, and Precision

  • Define accuracy and precision
  • Distinguish between exact and measured numbers
  • Use significant figures in calculations

Counting is the only type of measurement that is free from uncertainty, provided the number of objects being counted does not change while counting and the result is less than infinity.

Accuracy refers to how closely a measurement aligns with the correct or true value. Precision refers to how closely repeated measurements under unchanged conditions show the same results.

Significant figures convey the uncertainty in a measurement. The digits in a measurement include all certain digits plus one uncertain digit.

topics.title: Mathematical Treatment of Measurement Results

content_blocks.text: 1.6 Mathematical Treatment of Measurement Results
content_blocks.html: 1.6 Mathematical Treatment of Measurement Results

outcomes: Explain the factor-label method
Perform calculations involving unit conversions
Calculate density from mass and volume
content_blocks.html:
• Explain the factor-label method
  • Perform calculations involving unit conversions
  • Calculate density from mass and volume

content_blocks.text: Often it is necessary to convert a quantity from one unit to another. This is done using a method called dimensional analysis or the factor-label method.
content_blocks.html: Often it is necessary to convert a quantity from one unit to another. This is done using a method called dimensional analysis or the factor-label method.

content_blocks.text: Density is the ratio of mass to volume for a substance. The formula for density is: density = mass / volume.
content_blocks.html: Density is the ratio of mass to volume for a substance. The formula for density is: density = mass / volume.

content_blocks.text: Unit conversions require conversion factors, which are ratios of equivalent quantities expressed in different units.
content_blocks.html: Unit conversions require conversion factors, which are ratios of equivalent quantities expressed in different units.

Often it is necessary to convert a quantity from one unit to another. This is done using a method called dimensional analysis or the factor-label method.
